title: NASA's Orion Spacecraft Has a Small Leak on Its Moon Mission
summary: NASA's Orion spacecraft is on a mission to fly around the Moon with four astronauts. During the flight, engineers found a small leak of helium gas in the spacecraft's system. Helium is used to push fuel to the engines, but the leak is inside the valves and not going into space. This leak does not affect the spacecraft's ability to fly safely back to Earth. The astronauts are still on track to return home and splash down in the ocean. NASA is testing the system to learn more about the leak and decide if any changes are needed for future missions. The leaky part will be thrown away before the spacecraft reenters Earth's atmosphere, so it won't cause any problems during landing.

questions.question: Where will the Orion spacecraft land after its mission?

background_read: NASA is working on the Artemis program to send astronauts back to the Moon. The Orion spacecraft is a key part of this program, designed to carry crews on lunar missions. Helium is a gas used in spacecraft to help push fuel to the engines, making sure they work properly. Valves are important components that control how gases and liquids move in the spacecraft. When a spacecraft reenters Earth's atmosphere, it needs to be very careful to land safely, often by splashing down in the ocean. NASA tests everything to keep astronauts safe during space missions.
Article_Structure: WHO: NASA engineers and astronauts on the Orion spacecraft
WHAT: A small helium leak in the spacecraft's valves during a Moon mission
WHERE: In space, on the Orion spacecraft flying around the Moon
WHY: To understand the leak and ensure future missions are safe, even though it doesn't affect the current return to Earth

perspectives.description: NASA is testing the leak to make sure astronauts stay safe, even if it doesn't cause immediate danger.
